lg.l: [Thus] said the Sun. She [Kaṇṇaki] with shoulders [with] shining, cut bangles
Did not stay [silent]. Lifting [in her] hand the one anklet that remained,
Saying, ‘Oh wives - ladies possessing complete qualities, who live, staying,
[In] the town of the king without order [= justice], I suffered this one thing.
When I experienced a grief that was never experienced before,
I had that which was never had [before]. I have [= suffer] this one thing.
This one thing: they killed my husband - he is not a thief! -
For the matter of the price taken for the anklet of my foot. This one thing:
Will I [ever] see [my] beloved husband in front of all beautiful, proper, innocent ladies?
This one thing: If I see [my] beloved husband, will I hear from his mouth
Good words [of his being] faultless? If I go without hearing [his] faultless, good words,
Reproach [me], saying, ‘she created [this situation] of pain! This one thing.’
All the people of Maturai, having lamented, seeing her who cried inconsolably,
Having been possessed by sorrow, [they] being confused/in a state,
Showing to this lady of unremoveable distress, saying, ‘What is this!
The straight sceptre [of justice] that is unbending has bent!
What is this! The success of the Southern One - the king of kings,
[With] a moon[-like] parasol [and] sword has perished! What is this!
The cool parasol of the paragon [with] a righteous spear, who cools the earth,
[Now] produces heat! What is this! A new, great deity has come
For our sakes, lifting a singular anklet of red-gold [in her] hand!
She is apt [in her] mourning, crying [from] kajal-eyes [with] beautiful streaks.
What is this!’ Saying these things, despairing [and] lamenting,
As they comforted [her], the clamouring people of great Maturai
Of families that spread [the news of] the strong blame, showing [her]
Her husband[’s body], He [the sun], did not see her —
The lady who was like a red-gold creeper who saw [her husband’s body];
Having fed the big earth of the prosperous one [with] darkness,
Reducing [his] red, victorious rays upon a big mountain,
As the red sun, having disappeared, hid [himself], in the low, perturbed evening,
As the lady [like] a flower-creeper mourned, the town offered the sound of silence.
She who took, upon her overflowing tresses a garland from [his] dark hair-tuft
Around which bees swarmed, at [her] husband’s side in the morning,
[Now] in the evening, as blood flowing out from wounds,
She could not watch, he not having seen her [in her] intense grief.
Saying, ‘Even though you saw my great misery, you do not say
‘An affliction possesses her.’ Oh, [your] gold-like, fragrant body -
How it lies, mixed up [in] dirt! To I who do not know of the bold Karma
That was done by the great sin of the king, won’t they say
‘It is this, look’ — [about] my strong Karma!
Before me, full of pain, [in] the confusing evening without anyone [to help],
How it lies, [your] chest [with] sapphires, abundant with garlands,
Submerged [into] the ground! Won’t they say, ‘It is this, look! —
The Karma that is drawn out, as the world spreads great blame [on me],
As the Pāṇṭiyaṉ makes a mistake! Before me who possesses a cruel Karma
In which a flowing flood [from the eyes] pours forth,
How you lie, as one with blood that flows from wounds!
Won’t they say, ‘It is this, look! The Karma that consumes [her]’,
As humanity spreads blame, as the king makes a mistake!
Are there woman [here]? Are there women?
Are there women who must endure the fault done to the husbands they took?
Are there women? Are there noble people? Are there noble people?
Are there noble people who raise, having lifted [in their arms] the child they birthed?
Is there a God in Kūṭal of the king who failed with [his] sharp sword?
Is there a God?’ Saying these things she would cry. As she took, having embraced
Him with the chest upon which gold rests, he stood, rising,
Saying, ‘the bright face [like] a lustrous moon has aged [= faded].’
As [she with] choice jewels, having cried, lamented falling to the ground,
Grasped, worshipping with [her] hand [with] a pair of bangles
[His] flourishing (?), perfect feet, [He] leaving behind [his] injuries,
He rose. He who was now among the crowd of immortals,
Saying, ‘Oh [you with] eyes [with] kajal drawn on them, [with] the beauty
[Of] flowers, stay here!’, he left. She said, ‘Is this an illusion? I say!
Is it a God that confused [us]? As for an explanation of [this] matter,
Where do I go [and] search? Apart from this, unless [my] burning anger subsides,
I will not [be able to] join [my] hands [in respect] to [my] husband.
Having met with the evil king, I will go ask [him about] this matter.’
She who said [so] got up [from the ground]. She thought about, standing,
Of the evil dream that inflicted sorrow. She stood [there], as tears poured [from]
Long carp[-like] eyes. She who stood there, not wiping [those] tears
[From] long [carp-like] eyes, went before the entrance of the flourishing palace
